California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
John Glenn 
President 
August 25, 1988 
Bay Area Rapid Transit District Board 
800 Madison street 
P.O. Box 12688 
Oakland, CA 
94606-2688 
Dear Mr. Glenn: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-240 
I am writing to confirm our telephone conversation of 
August 23, 1988, at which time you withdrew your request for 
formal written advice, and requested, instead, this confirming 
letter regarding your ability to appoint BART Board Members to 
a liaison committee involved with the development of an 
agreement between BART and santa Clara County. 
As you are aware, the Political Reform Act prohibits any 
public official from participating in a governmental decision 
in which he or she has a financial interest. 
Because of your 
business dealings with Santa Clara County, you are prohibited 
from participating in decisions which would foreseeably have a 
material financial effect on Santa Clara County. 
The question presented is whether it is foreseeable that 
appointing members to the liaison committee will have a 
material financial effect on Santa Clara County. 
Absent any 
evidence that you have attempted in any way to influence the 
independent actions of the board members whom you would appoint 
to the liaison committee (see Benjamin Advice Letter, No. 
A-86-148, copy enclosed), and based on your description of the 
operation of the liaison committee, we do not feel the 
foreseeability test has been met. 
If you have any additional questions, please contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
LS:plh 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths

Telephone (415) 464-6000 
Ms. Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
June 23, 1988 
California Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street - Suite 800 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804-0807 
Dear Ms. Griffiths: 
Thank you very much for your courtesy and assistance in our 
two telephone conversations. 
I sincerely appreciate your 
permitting me to take part in the symbolic signing agreement 
between BART and SamTrans. 
To confirm what I told you, I did not take part in the 
negotiations. 
The vote by the eligible BART Board members 
was unanimous and the contract has been signed by the Vice 
President. 
Therefore, whatever I do now will not change 
that agreement • 
Pursuant to your request, I am providing the details for my 
request to appoint a BART Board Committee to bring to the 
Board any agreement which could be made with the Santa Clara 
County Transportation Agency for them to join the BART 
system. 
As I advised you, a firm which I own bid on a 
contract to service the liability claims of the Agency and 
won the contract as the low bidder. 
The BART President is authorized to appoint all Board 
Committees. 
The actual negotiations are conducted by the 
BART staff. 
The staff has worked out a formula in which any 
agency joining the system must pay the total local cost of 
what is built outside the District plus a pro rata share of 
what has already been constructed inside the District. 
This may be somewhat confusing to you, and I would be 
pleased to come to Sacramento with our General Manager so 
that he can explain how the above formula is applied, 
showing that neither I nor any other BART Director would 
have any control over changing that formula. 
All Board 
Directors are supportive of that formula. 


• 
Ms. Diane M. Griffiths 
Page 2 
June 23, 1988 
There is no way that I can personally benefit by 
appointing the Board Committee for the negotiating 
group. 
As far as my contract with Santa Clara County 
Transportation Agency, it is simply awarded to the 
lowest bidder and were I not to be the lowest bidder 
the next time the contract is let, I would lose it 
whether or not I appointed .the Committee. 
I respectfully request that as the unanimously elected 
BART President I be permitted to appoint the Directors 
to that Committee. 
I will not take part in the 
negotiations nor any debate nor will I vote on the 
matter. 
I will be pleased to come to Sacramento with 
our General Manager to prove to you that I would have 
no influence upon the outcome of any agreement that 
the BART Board would accept. 
Yours very truly, 
President 


JOHN GLENN

an attorney in the Legal Division.

regarding your letter, you may reach her at (916) 322-5901 • 
Sincerely,

sections.question: Whether it is foreseeable that appointing members to the liaison committee will have a material financial effect on Santa Clara County, given the requestor's business dealings with Santa Clara County.
sections.conclusion: Absent any evidence that the requestor has attempted in any way to influence the independent actions of the board members whom he would appoint to the liaison committee, and based on the description of the operation of the liaison committee, the foreseeability test has not been met. The requestor may appoint members to the liaison committee.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether BART President John Glenn, who owns a firm with a contract to service liability claims for Santa Clara County Transportation Agency, can appoint BART Board Members to a liaison committee involved in developing an agreement between BART and Santa Clara County without violating the Political Reform Act's prohibition on participating in decisions with foreseeable material financial effects.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the foreseeability test under the Political Reform Act is not met because there is no evidence of attempted influence over appointed board members and the liaison committee has no autonomous decision-making authority. Therefore, the requestor may appoint BART Board Members to the liaison committee despite his financial interest with Santa Clara County.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letter exchanges including incoming requests (June 23, August 5) and the formal FPPC response (August 25, 1988). The requestor withdrew his formal advice request and instead requested a confirming letter. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers, but question and conclusion content are clearly identifiable from the August 25 FPPC letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether it is foreseeable that appointing members to the liaison committee will have a material financial effect on Santa Clara County, given the requestor's business dealings with Santa Clara County.

CONCLUSION: Absent any evidence that the requestor has attempted in any way to influence the independent actions of the board members whom he would appoint to the liaison committee, and based on the description of the operation of the liaison committee, the foreseeability test has not been met. The requestor may appoint members to the liaison committee.

As you are aware, the Political Reform Act prohibits any public official from participating in a governmental decision in which he or she has a financial interest. Because of your business dealings with Santa Clara County, you are prohibited from participating in decisions which would foreseeably have a material financial effect on Santa Clara County. The question presented is whether it is foreseeable that appointing members to the liaison committee will have a material financial effect on Santa Clara County. Absent any evidence that you have attempted in any way to influence the independent actions of the board members whom you would appoint to the liaison committee (see Benjamin Advice Letter, No. A-86-148, copy enclosed), and based on your description of the operation of the liaison committee, we do not feel the foreseeability test has been met. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to BART President John Glenn's withdrawal of a formal request, confirming via letter his ability to appoint BART Board Members to a liaison committee for BART-Santa Clara County negotiations despite his ownership of a firm with a liability claims contract with Santa Clara County Transportation Agency. The FPPC determined the foreseeability test was not met under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions.
